speaker: interviewer
text: --maiden name is Wos. Let's pick up on the information about Kristallnacht that you had heard. Could you describe whatever you had heard at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard about Kristallnacht. And opinion was very bad about German. And probably, Polish government was prepared that could be something like that for Polish people in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think the risk was of-- for your family to be involved with any Jewish people who needed help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh-- what risk? Of, of course everybody knew that the risk to help Jewish people was, was, like, [SIGHS] be shot if somebody will know. This is death sentence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any family friends who were involved that way with Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you now. I don't remember. Maybe yes, but I, I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever talk about the risk to his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know because it was in newspaper which German government gave to Poland. If somebody help Jewish people, will be shot on spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that your father took food into the ghetto when he had to go to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see, it's quite understanding-- I think so. If somebody's hungry, I have to give him something. This is humanity. And it should be so. And we were brought up in this atmosphere and thinking that it should be so. We can't be egoistic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of news did you get from your radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it's too difficult now to-- [LAUGHS] to talk about it. It's so many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Radio Free Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I knew that it was a secret newspaper, small bulletins, between people from Underground Army to-- given to all Polish. But it, it should be very, very careful given because so many people are not-- nobody knows what they could do. Some people, for money, could sell other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke about a Pole that-- who was helping a Jew. Would you describe that incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, this man? [SIGHS] Who, who had hidden this boy? This man was a good man, maybe about 20 or 30 years old. He was, like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] working in, in the office somewhere. But he was very good Polish, very good patriot, and also human being. He agreed to help this boy. And I'm sure it was not only one. It was so many in Warsaw. Many people survived because of this kind of help. He was alone. I know that it was difficult to to kept in secret, especially behind this closet to give him food, to clean. But it was hero. He loves life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the boy in the closet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that it was about one year, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about one year. I was, couple times, there-- what I could help him. I-- I could only brought some book or maybe cookies. That's it because we didn't have too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you felt when you were going there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sad, very afraid that if somebody can see, maybe, maybe somebody who, who can watch. It was risky, very risky. But I was very happy to see him. I saw that he's so smart, so intelligent. [SIGHS] And I told you that news that he lost life was devastating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe your first meeting with the AK Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: First meeting was in the small group. And officer took oath from us that we will be always [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] work and be for Poland, and Polish, and fight for freedom. But Underground Army was not political organization, only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for freedom fighting. And in Polish Underground Army, I know there was many Jewish people also fighting together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my group was only my two girlfriend from school. One named Wanda, but I forgot last name. And the other was Mischka Biedermann, whose father was arrested and killed by Nazis, and two, two sister-- older sister was in concentration camp in Majdanek in this time. And she was the last one. And she was, was my closest friend. And we went to these meetings. They show us how to work with wounded people, first aid, not too much I knew [LAUGHS] this time. But it was idea. And our-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we want to help. We want to fight to be free for, for our country this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you tell that you were at the meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? She-- my girlfriend Mischka Biedermann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who in your family did you tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody, because it was secret. I told you before that we couldn't talk about it. This is finished-- I knew only three person and one instructor, who was unknown for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this oath took-- one, one officer, I remember his name, [NON-ENGLISH]. But he's-- but he passed away many, many years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And only this Mischka, my girlfriend-- and now she's living in Poland. She's bacteriologist. I saw her. But she doesn't want to talk about it. You see, some people prefer to forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: How frequently were your meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I think that was every couple days in different places, not in one place. Because it could be suspicious, young girls coming in group. What for? It was very difficult to, to keep secret in, in Warsaw this time. Because we had many people who are watching only, maybe something they can make money or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your parents think you were going when you went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always, I was lying [LAUGHS] that I'm going to my additional class of geography. Or I'm going to make homework with my girlfriend. If you want, you can lie. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: For how many years did you do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too-- not too long time because when I joined anti-- oath in army, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after a couple months, it start to be uprising-- 1st of August, yeah, 1944. We finished this education about nursing, very simple nursing. And next should be how to handle these weapons with ammunition. Because these girls was not only for first help, but also as a, a [SIGHS] courier to, to be between officers and-- on barricades. It doesn't mean it was many process to, to learn. You see-- and before second teaching, get uprising. I was not in my group because lack of communication. And I stay in this place where my parents were. And this-- if you want to talk about the uprising-- it happened on Friday, 1st of August, in 1944. We have 30-something on the street. I couldn't move to place where I should be with my group. I stay in [NON-ENGLISH] street. Then, in our basement, because it was a very big basement under this church, this Greco-Catholic church, they brought wounded boys. Boys because uprising was mostly young people. They brought-- and gradually, when bombarding finished with buildings, everybody was in basements because no place. Our house also was destroyed. And these wounded people came. They were brought to basement. I helped them, changing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this gauze on the wound. But it was very simple, lack of material for nursing. I remember that my mother brought our linen from upstair because was destroyed, this house. And we cut for small pieces as bandages. Then we use it. I-- my, my hand-- my book-- not book, my sock like this one from school, I use as first aid. I put a Red Cross. And I was running from basement to basement to help because many people was-- who were very wounded and very sick. My knowledge of this, it was [LAUGHS] not too good. [LAUGHS] I-- now I can smile only how I did. But I wanted to help. It was-- it the-- it was till moment when uprising, this part of town, old town, was taken by German people, German soldier. But mostly, it was not German-- Lithuanian or Ukrainian, who joined on the side of Germany. They came like animal, probably under influence of something, alcohol or drugs. Because they were so cruel. They took everybody from this basement. In the meantime, soldier will put to canal-- to canal-- you know what is it-- to other-- to center Warsaw, wounded or not. And soldier left. I didn't want to go to other part of Warsaw because my mother was already devastated with knowing that my brother is in [NON-ENGLISH] Center. I stay with my parents. And in this moment when these groups came, they start to shoot people. If somebody was wounded, they shoot in the spot. And everybody who could go was taken out in big column. And we-- we-- we took only with us whatever you can-- everybody can keep in their hand. My mother was very smart. And she gave-- they knew that something could happen. And she gave us gold to put all over because she knew that gold could be helpful to survive. We were taken to Pruszkow, close to Warsaw. And then came order. Immediately, everybody-- because are bandits from uprising-- to concentration camp. They put us-- put us to very crowded cattle train-- not train-- no, wagons. And journey start to be. Or it was on September 1st, or something. It doesn't mean-- I was one month in this hell, very close to death, very close to shot, very close. Very often I was-- like, maybe angel was with me, [LAUGHS] guardian angel. And, you know, when-- they took us with these wagons to, to Flossenburg. When we left this wagon-- very crowded, all my family was with us. And we suppose that will be some, like for human being, but no. They put us very bad weather, couple of kilometer on foot, very hungry, very upset, [LAUGHS] and very unhappy. They took us to the big, big place, which greeted us with sign, Arbeit macht frei, you see, big nice sign. And we came to the-- to Flossenburg. It was terrible experience to see only this block, these barracks. And some people going in striped dress very, very frightened, hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people, they look at us very interested, what-- what's happened? Because this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this camp was only for men. And then they said us, dress out, strip off, and go to shower. Shower, of course, everybody not with men. Men is separately. We were separately.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to this cold shower, undress with our valuables-- valuable things we left. Probably, they said it will be for to kill some animals. [LAUGHS] And, you see, when we left, we, we received new dresses. Each was striped on the back with other color, and nothing much, of course. And we look like from circus, but not so important. Men were separate, women separated. After two weeks of struggling there, they sent us again to other. And then I left my brother and father in Flossenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you on the transport before you got to Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father; mother; aunt; her friend, old lady; our two maids, our, our chief from factory, who, who cut this material, or was old lady. But she was always working in my father's store because she came to my parents. She lost son in uprising. And so many people was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Irena, your sister, with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And sister, of course. Only time I was with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us the names of the maids and your aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt, my mother sister, Sonia Szachowska. I spelled to you before, yes? My-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot this. Paulina is friend of my aunt. I forgot name. But she lost life in Ravensbruck, and she was burned. And two maids. One was [? Anyela ?]. I don't remember last name. And other, [? Janka ?] [INAUDIBLE] She, she was all the time in concentration camp with us. My mother, of course, my aunt, and this lady, they stay up to the end in Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we continue with what happened to you after Flossenburg, when you were involved in the uprising in Warsaw, were you aware of any activities taking place at the Warsaw ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I mentioned to you before that I knew that underground army is helping Jewish people to, to supply in ammunition, to-- taking some children to, to safe places. And very, very close to me, it was problem-- not problem, action of this group where my, my and my brother friend lost life-- action of-- on Street Bonifraterska. It was street very-- this is street close to ghetto. And this action had goal to put dynamite and ruin this wall. Not too much, but enough to withdraw people from ghetto because everybody knew that ghetto is going to, to be finished by German. To--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody knew what's happened in the-- in the middle of our town. Everybody knew that German-- and they, they told also, this is last-- we have to finish with Jewish. They took-- every day, they took big transport to Treblinka, I don't know-- to Majdanek. I don't know, really. In this last transportation, Mrs. Melamed, this beautiful girl, was lost. Her mother, she took this girl with her. And they was-- is in gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war-- after the war. When we return from concentration camp, Mrs. Melamed tried to find us because she was in Jerusalem. And from her, we know that the daughter lost life and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also had previously mentioned that you knew a family by the name of [? Epstein. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably my, my brother knows better [? Epstein. ?] I remember Neufeld and Melamed. Neufeld-- I told you, this man without arm, and Melamed family which were on friend-- friendship with my, my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get back to what happened to you. When you arrived in Flossenburg, where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks was quite big, on three bunks, not wooden floor, only, like, dust floor, one-- each bunk was filled with wooden pieces, not even material, only in paper. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what's happened? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was there only two weeks because we were transported. Is barracks-- three bunks. And they each would have only to cover very thin something-- from material, but very thin. Cold, sad, no, no soap, nothing, very bad looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the toilets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toilets, usually between-- in the middle, was a row not cover with doors, only sitting, for sitting place. And across the-- this wall was a place where we can wash. Water was very rare and cold only, never warm-- very dark, very upsetting.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to do all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it was very difficult because [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I, I never have idea-- had idea, even, how it-- it could be. And especially when I came to Flossenburg, I had broken skin. And it start to be pus and dirt. Well, it was very depressing. We hoped that maybe later will be better. But when we lost presence of my parents-- my father and brother, we were very depressed. Only, I, I was so happy that I found one boy, as I mention you before, who was arrested on the beginning of the war from Poznan. And he, he was working as helper in-- not hospital, it's, it's only place where they help people. And he promised me to help, a little, my father. Maybe, therefore, they, they, they have-- still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this friend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Janus Janewski. He was living in Poznan, very close to German-- Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Right now, he's surgeon, but-- a surgeon in Poznan, also, but very sick person. He couldn't leave Poland now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did you get in Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, in each-- in each campus, we received about 200 grams. It doesn't mean-- I tell you this-- [POINTS] and maybe thicker or this bread. This bread was very often with mold or heavy, like cement.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue this on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
